skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automate Twitter/X posting, engagement, and user management through the inference.sh belt CLI. Run apps like x/post-tweet, x/post-create, x/post-like, x/post-retweet, x/dm-send, and x/user-follow to publish tweets, schedule content, like and retweet posts, send direct messages, follow users, and fetch profiles.
skill.summary: Automate Twitter/X posting, engagement, and user actions through the inference.sh belt CLI.
skill.author: inference-sh-skills

security_audit.summary: The skill is a Markdown documentation file describing how to call Twitter/X automation apps through the inference.sh belt CLI. Static analyzer findings for 'external_commands' and 'network' are false positives: the matches are documentation code blocks and HTTPS links to inference.sh and GitHub, not executable scripts. The two HIGH 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings (lines 3 and 29) are also false positives, triggered by table syntax and the YAML description, not real cryptography. No prompt injection, exfiltration, or malicious patterns were identified.

low_findings.title: Documented shell commands invoke the belt CLI
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains many bash code blocks invoking the belt CLI (for example belt login, belt app run x/post-tweet). These are documentation snippets executed by the user, not embedded scripts that run automatically. Risk is limited to the scope of the belt CLI and the configured Twitter/X account.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly documentation examples in fenced code blocks. They are real CLI calls a user would run, so they carry normal social-media automation risk, but no hidden or obfuscated behavior.

low_findings.title: External documentation and image URLs
low_findings.description: Hardcoded HTTPS links to inference.sh, cloud.inference.sh, and raw.githubusercontent.com are present. They point to documentation and a banner image, not data exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs target the skill vendor's official domains and GitHub raw content. There is no evidence of exfiltration or contact with unknown endpoints.

content.user_title: Automate Twitter and X with the Belt CLI
content.value_statement: Manual posting, liking, and messaging on Twitter and X is slow and hard to scale across many accounts and workflows. This skill lets Claude, Codex, and Claude Code drive the inference.sh belt CLI to post tweets, attach media, like, retweet, send direct messages, follow users, and fetch profiles with simple commands.
actual_capabilities: Post text tweets through the x/post-tweet app.
Publish tweets with image or video media through the x/post-create app.
Like, retweet, get, and delete tweets by tweet ID.
Send direct messages to a recipient through x/dm-send.
Follow users and fetch user profiles through x/user-follow and x/user-get.
Chain image or video generation apps with a post-create call to publish AI-generated media.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI to be installed and the user to be logged in.
Requires a connected Twitter or X account with valid API access.
Does not bypass Twitter or X rate limits and content policy rules.
Provides no built-in scheduler. Recurring posts need an external job runner.

use_cases.title: Publish product launch updates
use_cases.description: A product team posts launch announcements and follow-up tweets from a release script that calls belt app run x/post-tweet.

use_cases.title: Share AI-generated media
use_cases.description: A creator generates an image or video with another inference.sh app and posts it to X with a single x/post-create command.

use_cases.title: Run a community engagement bot
use_cases.description: A community manager scripts likes, retweets, and welcome direct messages for new followers using x/post-like, x/post-retweet, and x/dm-send.

prompt_templates.title: Post a simple tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the twitter-automation skill to post the following tweet for me: "Shipping a new feature today." Confirm when it is posted.

prompt_templates.title: Post a tweet with an image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the twitter-automation skill to post a tweet with this caption and image URL. Caption: "Our latest design preview." Image URL: https://example.com/preview.png

prompt_templates.title: Engage with a tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Using twitter-automation, like the tweet with ID 1234567890, then retweet it, and finally fetch the tweet details so I can review the metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Generate and post AI media
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the inference.sh belt CLI, generate an image with the prompt "sunset over mountains," then post it to X with the caption "AI-generated sunset." Return the new tweet URL.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: chained generation and posting workflow

output_examples.input: Post a tweet that says "Hello from inference.sh!"
output_examples.output: Tweet posted successfully through the x/post-tweet app. The tweet text is "Hello from inference.sh!" and the response includes the new tweet identifier returned by the belt CLI.

output_examples.input: Like the tweet with ID 1234567890 and then retweet it.
output_examples.output: The x/post-like app confirms a like action on tweet 1234567890. The x/post-retweet app then confirms a retweet on the same tweet. Both responses include status fields returned by the belt CLI.

output_examples.input: Send a direct message to user_id_here saying "Thanks for the follow."
output_examples.output: The x/dm-send app reports that a direct message was sent to recipient user_id_here. The message body is "Thanks for the follow." and the response includes a delivery status from the belt CLI.

best_practices: Log in once with belt login and verify the active account before running automated posting.
Keep tweet text, recipient IDs, and media URLs as variables in your prompt so values are easy to audit.
Test new workflows on a sandbox or staging X account before running them on a production handle.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for spam, mass following, or other actions that violate Twitter and X policies.
Do not store API tokens in the skill files. Rely on the belt CLI login session.
Do not loop posting commands without delays. This can trigger rate limits and account suspension.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It documents how to call inference.sh belt CLI apps that automate Twitter and X actions such as posting, liking, retweeting, direct messaging, and following.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The skill is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. Any of these tools can run the belt CLI commands shown in the documentation.

faq.question: Do I need a Twitter or X developer account?
faq.answer: You need a Twitter or X account that is connected to inference.sh and has the API access required by the belt apps you plan to use.

faq.question: Can this skill schedule tweets?
faq.answer: The skill itself does not include a scheduler. You can schedule posts by calling the belt commands from your own cron job, CI pipeline, or workflow runner.

faq.question: Can it post images and videos?
faq.answer: Yes. The x/post-create app accepts a media_url field, and the documentation shows how to chain image or video generation apps before posting.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is a Markdown documentation file with CLI examples. It contains no embedded scripts, network calls, or credential handling beyond the user-driven belt CLI.
